gps.title: GPS delay
first-pagaraphs: The information shown on maps is based on the GPS data received from tags which is usually subject to delays of around 10 to 15 minutes.
Delays vary depending on network coverage and how often tags upload GPS data.
Use timestamps to find out when and how long a person on probation visited a location.

loi.title: Locations of interest

image.alt: Map showing a location of interest

first-pagaraphs: As a person on probation enters or leaves a location of interest this movement is recorded in their location history and on their map.
Locations of interest have defined boundaries which reflect the real-world and include:
bulletPoints: places of interest - for example, pubs
required locations - for example, places of work and training providers
last-pagaraphs: When you view a location of interest on the map, you’ll be able to check where the person was before and after they visited the location.
The map will include the person’s GPS trail from around 10 minutes before to around 10 minutes after they were detected at that location.
If you want to view more of their GPS trail, you can adjust the timings using the filter above the map.

confidenceCircles.title: Confidence circles
first-pagaraphs: The centre of a circle shows a person on probation’s most likely location.
Circle size reflects the accuracy of GPS data and our level of confidence about the person on probation’s exact location. For example:
firstBulletPoints: smaller circles = more confidence about a location
larger circles = less confidence about a location
last-pagaraphs: Various factors affect the accuracy of GPS data and the size of confidence circles including:

linesAndArrows.title: Lines and arrows
first-pagaraphs: The lines on maps show the likely path a person on probation took between locations and do not represent a confirmed route.
They only estimate and give a general sense of the person on probation's movement over time.
The arrows on lines show a person on probation's direction of travel.
